text: Everything that is wrong in my life goes back to my mother. She made me who I am and not in a good way. I can trace every bad relationship, every bad decision, every way I'm broken directly back to what she did and didn't do when I was growing up.
My ex ruined me. I was a normal person before her. I had confidence, I had friends, I had a future. She destroyed all of that and now I'm supposed to just rebuild myself? Because of what she did?

I can't be happy in relationships because of him. He was my first serious partner and he showed me that love means being controlled and criticized. That's his fault. I carry that into every relationship now because of what he did.

My dad left when I was seven and that's why I have abandonment issues. That's why I push people away. That's why I can't stay in a relationship. I know that. I understand the cause. But understanding the cause doesn't fix the damage he did.

My coworker sabotaged my project and I got a bad review because of her. She knew what she was doing. The whole performance rating cycle is ruined because of one person's deliberate actions against me.
I'm this way because of what happened to me. I didn't choose this. I didn't choose to be anxious, to shut down, to push people away. Other people did things to me that made me like this.

It's my fault. Everything is my fault. My partner is miserable and it's because of me. My kids see a dysfunctional marriage and that's on me. Everything that is wrong in my house traces back to my failures.
My best friend told me she needed space and I can't stop going over everything I did wrong. I must have been a bad friend in ways I didn't notice. The distance she's putting between us is my fault.

If my parents had just been consistent, just slightly better parents, I would not have the problems I have. They handed me these wounds and now I'm supposed to carry them alone as an adult because they couldn't be bothered to parent properly.
My brother went down the wrong path in life and I was the older sibling. I should have looked out for him more. What happened to him is something I could have prevented. That's on me.

He makes me feel small every single time we talk. He doesn't even have to say anything specific. Just the way he looks at me, or doesn't look at me. My mood for an entire day is determined by a five-minute interaction with him.
I feel awful about myself because of what she said at the party. I know I should let it go. I can't. She said it in front of people. She knew what she was doing. She wanted me to feel this way.
I'm terrible at my job and I know it. I'm the weak link on the team. I make mistakes that other people have to clean up. It's my fault that the project is behind.

She knew I had trust issues when she got into this relationship with me. She chose to be with me knowing that. And then she did the exact thing that would break my trust. That's not an accident. That's her fault.

I can't function properly because of my childhood. I know that's not an excuse but it is an explanation. The adults in my life failed me completely and I have been dealing with the consequences my entire adult life.
I'm the reason my marriage fell apart. I know that. My therapist wants me to be less hard on myself but how do I do that when it's true? I was avoidant. I was distant. I pushed her away.

My manager gave me an impossible workload and then blamed me when I couldn't deliver. She set me up to fail. I tried to tell her the timeline was unrealistic and she ignored me. My poor performance review is entirely her doing.
I stopped trusting people because people in my life showed me what happens when you trust. I didn't become this way on my own. People taught me this through what they did.

My sister has always been the favorite and my parents have never tried to hide it. The way I feel about myself, the way I compare myself to others, the way I always feel like I'm not enough — that came from watching her be chosen over me my whole childhood.

I ruined the friendship. I know I did. I said things I shouldn't have and I didn't listen the way I should have. When she pulled away that was a consequence of my behavior, not something she just did randomly.

My ex left me and started dating someone new almost immediately. She made me feel worthless and replaceable. The way I feel about relationships now, about my own value, is a direct result of how she handled our breakup.

I sabotage good things in my life and I've traced it back to my father. He would take away anything I was enjoying when he was in a bad mood. I learned that good things get taken away. It's easier to destroy them yourself before that happens.

I'm exhausted and burned out and I know exactly why. My boss refuses to let me take time off even when I have it accrued. She piles on tasks at the end of the day. She is directly responsible for how I feel.
I can't be in large groups anymore and it goes back to being mocked in school. Those kids made me afraid of groups of people. I carry that everywhere I go as an adult.

She left and I fell apart. My whole life depended on the relationship and when it ended I had nothing. I know that's on me, I know I shouldn't have made her my whole life, but she also knew what I was like and she left anyway.

My anxiety is my father's legacy to me. Decades of watching him panic over nothing, modeling that fear response, teaching me that the world is dangerous. That's where my anxiety comes from. He gave it to me.

I'm the worst. I'm the reason my kids are struggling. I've modeled anxiety and avoidance their whole lives. My therapist says I'm being too hard on myself. She doesn't understand. This is just accurate self-assessment.
My partner's unhappiness is my responsibility. When she's having a bad day, it's usually because of something I did or didn't do. I can see it even when she says it's not about me. I know better.
I blew the opportunity and that's entirely on me. I was not prepared enough. I didn't do what I should have done and when it mattered I failed and now the opportunity is gone because of my failure.

My ex-best friend told everyone personal things I had shared with her. She violated my trust publicly. I trusted her completely and she used it against me. I have not been able to be close to anyone since then. That's her fault.

I've had a hard life and I know who is responsible. My parents made choices that put us in poverty, put us in dangerous neighborhoods, put us in situations no kid should be in. The instability I grew up with shaped me. They did that.
I missed the window on so many things. Career, relationships, education. I spent my twenties in survival mode because of how chaotic my family was. They stole that decade from me.

My team missed the deadline and I know why. My colleague kept changing requirements and wouldn't make decisions. Everything that went wrong in that project was downstream of her indecisiveness. It falls on her.

I cope badly with stress and I understand where it comes from. I grew up in a house where stress was never managed, just dumped onto children. My parents didn't handle their own stress and I never learned how. They are responsible for that gap.

I'm in therapy trying to unlearn things that my mother spent twenty years drilling into me. Every session I work on something she installed in me. The amount of damage one person can do to their child is staggering.
My kids saw me and their father fight for years. I chose to stay longer than I should have. What they witnessed is because I didn't leave sooner. That's on me.

He dismissed every feeling I ever had for years and now I don't know how to trust my own emotional responses. I second-guess everything I feel. He took my ability to trust myself and I don't know how to get it back.

The reason I have no savings is because of how I was raised. We never had money, nobody modeled financial health, I had no education about it. The financial mess I'm in is a product of that upbringing.
I made everyone in my family feel unsafe with my drinking. I know that. The things my kids saw during those years are my fault. I can get sober but I can't undo what they experienced because of me.

My professor unfairly failed me on that assignment. I've read the rubric and my work met the criteria. She has never liked me and this is a pattern. My grade in this course is being sabotaged by her bias against me.
I can't be a good parent because I didn't have good parents. I'm parenting from a deficit. Whatever damage I do to my kids, the origin of it is the family I came from.

I ended the friendship and I know why. She was negative about everything I did. Every time I made progress she found something wrong with it. You can't build a life around someone who tears down everything you try to build.
I became an angry person and I know who's responsible. My father was angry and explosive and I watched him use that anger to control everyone around him. I absorbed that. He gave me his anger.

I'm the reason my friend group drifted apart. I was going through a hard time and I pulled away. When things stabilized I didn't re-engage the way I should have. The group isn't what it was and that's because of me.

I am so hard on myself and I have figured out where it comes from. My mother criticized everything I did. Nothing was ever good enough. I have her voice in my head criticizing me still. She put it there.

He cheated and then acted like the hurt feelings were the real problem. Like me being devastated was an overreaction. Like I had done something wrong by trusting him. He caused everything that happened and then made himself the victim.

I think about my childhood therapist who missed something obvious and reported nothing. I fell through every crack that should have caught me. The people whose job was to help me failed me completely.

I keep getting into the same types of relationships over and over and I've started to think it might be me. But also the common denominator keeps being people who are kind at first and then cruel. Is it me for trusting them, or is it them for lying?

I should have finished my degree. I know that. I dropped out and that derailed my entire career trajectory. That decision is the cause of most of the professional problems I'm dealing with now. I made that choice and I've been paying for it.

My roommate's lifestyle is affecting my sleep and therefore my performance at work. The complaints from my boss trace back to my sleep, which traces back to my roommate. The professional consequences are downstream of his choices.
I've become someone I don't recognize and I know when it started. It started when she died. I was functional before that. Whatever I am now came from that loss. Grief did this to me.
I isolated myself after the diagnosis and I know that made everything worse. My relationships atrophied because of my choices. I can't put that on anyone else.
He treated me like a burden and eventually I became one. If you tell someone long enough that they're difficult to love, they'll stop trying to be lovable. That's what he did to me.

My depression has hurt people I love. I've been absent, unreliable, difficult. I know my condition contributes but I'm also the one who has been those things. I'm responsible for the impact even if I didn't intend it.

I gave that person the benefit of the doubt when I shouldn't have. I ignored the red flags. Whatever happened to me as a result of trusting them, I have to acknowledge that I made the choice to trust.

My ex made me feel worthless for years and I believed it. I still believe it on some level. That's not something I did to myself. He told me I was worthless every day until it became what I believed about myself.

I am responsible for the distance in my family. I know that. I've been so wrapped up in my own struggles that I've been unavailable. My kids have noticed. My partner has felt alone. That's because of me.
The reason I don't stand up for myself is because I was taught not to. My mother taught me that expressing needs was selfish. That lesson has followed me everywhere.

I chose a field that doesn't pay well and I'm struggling financially. I made that choice when I was eighteen. I chose passion over practicality and I'm the one dealing with the consequences of that choice now.

My friend's mental health deteriorated partly because she was leaning on me and I wasn't there for her the way I should have been. When she finally got help she mentioned that my pulling back had hurt her. That's something I did.

I grew up in a home where my worth was tied to my performance. My parents loved me conditionally. Every perfectionist tendency I have, every time I can't just rest, that came from them. They created this.
The company tanked because our CEO made a series of bad calls. The investors pulled out because of his decisions. Three hundred people lost their jobs because of one man's poor judgment.
I was emotionally cruel to my partner during the worst of my illness and I said things I can't take back. She forgave me but I know those things are still there. I put them there.

My childhood robbed me of the ability to feel safe. I spend enormous amounts of energy just trying to feel okay in environments where other people are completely comfortable. That is not my fault. That's what happened to me.

I have a hard time finishing things and I know why. Every time I got excited about something as a kid my father would make fun of it. I learned to abandon interests before they could be taken from me.

I am the reason my previous relationship ended. I wouldn't let her in. I kept her at a distance until she gave up. I know I did that. She did the right thing by leaving someone who wouldn't open up to her.
The friend group stopped including me and I know I caused it. I was unreliable for a long time, I cancelled plans, I disappeared for weeks. Eventually people stop inviting you. I did that to myself.
My kids are anxious children and I know where they got it from. They got it from watching me be anxious. I gave them my anxiety the same way my mother gave it to me.
I had a breakdown at work in front of my whole team. I know that changed how they see me. It was a moment of weakness and it happened publicly. I did that.
I am why my family is broken. I'm the one who couldn't keep it together. I'm the one who needed too much. I'm the one who made everything harder. The dysfunction in my family revolves around me.
My partner has to manage my emotions on top of everything else in her life. That burden falls on her because of who I am. I drain her. I know that. She doesn't say it but I can see it.
I am responsible for my mother's unhappiness. She gave up a lot for me and I didn't turn out the way she needed me to. Her disappointment in her life is connected to her disappointment in me.
I lost my temper with my kids last night and I hate myself for it. I said things a parent shouldn't say. They deserve better. What they experienced last night was my failure, not just a bad moment.

My colleague takes credit for everything I do and when I don't push back it looks like I have nothing to contribute. I've let this go on for two years because I didn't know how to address it. My reputation is suffering because of her behavior and my inaction.
I know my depression affects my partner. When I'm low, the whole house shifts. She adjusts her behavior around my moods. She's walking on eggshells and that's because of me.
The abuse shaped everything. My attachment style, my responses to conflict, my inability to ask for help. Everything that makes my relationships hard now is downstream of what someone else did to me.

I got fired because my supervisor wanted to replace me with someone cheaper. She documented minor infractions and built a case over months. I lost my job not because of anything I did but because of a calculated decision she made.
My friend group doesn't know the real me because I never let them. That's my fault. They'd probably like me if I let them in, but I don't know how to do that anymore.

I grew up believing I had to earn love and that came from my parents. Love was transactional in my family. I know I bring that into every relationship. They put that belief there and now I can't get it out.
I could have stopped him from doing what he did. I saw signs and didn't act. Whatever came after is partly my responsibility. I knew something was wrong.
My therapist asks me to stop blaming myself but it's not blame, it's fact. I made the calls that led to the outcomes. Saying it's not my fault doesn't change the chain of causation.
He told me I was too needy and too much and for years I believed him. I tried to need less. I tried to be less. Now I don't know how to need anything from anyone. He did that.

I'm not good at conflict and I know exactly where it comes from. My parents fought constantly and loudly. Conflict meant danger in my house. I avoided it as survival. I still avoid it. They created that reflex in me.
I relapsed and I know why. I stopped going to meetings because of my work schedule. I made the schedule a priority over my recovery. I did that. This relapse is on me.

My marriage fell apart and I'm trying to be honest about what my role was. I was not emotionally available. I worked too much. I prioritized everything over my partner. She didn't leave for no reason. She left because of how I showed up.

The car accident wasn't my fault. The other driver ran the light. But the person in my car was hurt and they were there because I'd asked them to come with me. If I hadn't asked, they wouldn't have been in that car. That's something I carry.
I was the one who pushed the group into making that decision and it went badly. Everyone else had doubts. I argued them out of it. What happened after is something I pushed for and now I have to own.

My manager micromanages everything and it's destroying my ability to do my job. I've started second-guessing every decision because I know she'll question it anyway. My decline in performance is directly linked to her management style.
I was emotionally abusive when I was younger. I know that now. I said things to people I cared about that I cannot undo. Those people carry what I said to them. That is my responsibility.

He broke off the engagement without warning and I never fully recovered from that. The way I am in relationships now, the way I hold back and wait for people to leave, started that day. He did that to me.

The school never addressed the bullying. Teachers saw it and looked away. Administrators knew and did nothing. The damage that was done to me in those years was allowed to happen by every adult in that building who chose not to act.

I feel everything my partner feels. When she's stressed, I'm stressed. When she's in pain, I can't function. I know this isn't healthy but I feel like her emotional state is something that happens to me. I can't separate us.

My insomnia started the year I worked for him. The constant pressure, the impossible expectations, the humiliation in front of the team when something went wrong. My sleep has never recovered. That was his environment and he built it deliberately.
I ended the relationship and I know I hurt him. I could have ended it sooner and with more kindness. I let it drag on and that was its own form of cruelty. That's on me.

My chronic pain limits my ability to work and maintain relationships and take care of myself. My doctor made a diagnostic error that delayed treatment for two years. The progression of my condition during that time is because of that error.
I've spent years trying to make up for something I did when I was twenty-two. I hurt someone badly and they never fully forgave me. I don't fully forgive myself either. That action belongs to me.

My relationship with food is broken and it comes from my mother policing everything I ate as a child. She created a battlefield out of every meal. Every difficult relationship I have with eating traces back to her dinner table.

The team failed the client because of one person who refused to follow the process. Everyone else did their part. One person's refusal to cooperate cost all of us our professional reputation with that client.
I know I'm hard to be with. I know I demand too much. My partner says she doesn't feel that way but I can see it affecting her. That's my fault. My needs are more than she signed up for.

I have nightmares every night. I wake up with my heart pounding. My nervous system is wrecked. It's wrecked because of what was done to me. My body's responses are the proof of what someone else caused.
I've hurt people who loved me. Not intentionally, but the impact is the same as if it had been. The fact that I was struggling doesn't erase what they experienced because of how I behaved.

My father's drinking made our household unpredictable and terrifying for most of my childhood. The hypervigilance I carry everywhere — always watching for the next explosion — came from living with him.

I hate myself for what I did and I can't let it go. My therapist says I need to practice self-compassion but there's a difference between self-compassion and letting yourself off the hook for something genuinely bad that you did.

I've apologized multiple times but the person I hurt still hasn't forgiven me. At some point I have to accept that I did something that damaged a relationship permanently and no amount of apology will fix what I broke.
My anxiety cost me a relationship with someone I genuinely loved. I pushed her away because I couldn't tolerate closeness. I did that. She didn't leave because of anything she did.

The system failed me. Social services, the courts, the schools, every institution that was supposed to catch children in situations like mine looked the other way or was too slow. The damage I carry is partly institutional negligence.
I know I'm the reason I don't have closer friendships. I keep people at a comfortable distance. When they try to get closer I find a reason to push back. I do that. I'm aware that I do it.

He knew I was fragile when we got together. He knew my history. He chose to be careless with that anyway. The particular way that relationship ended did damage that I don't think was unavoidable. He could have chosen differently.
I ended up in a manipulative relationship because I didn't value myself enough to leave early. I saw the signs and stayed. That was my choice even if the manipulation made it hard to see clearly.
My emotional unavailability comes from somewhere. It comes from a father who modeled stoicism as strength and punished any vulnerability. He created that pattern in me.
She's unhappy in the relationship and I feel responsible. She has told me the things she needs that I haven't provided. Her unhappiness is a direct response to what I'm not giving her.
I became the person I swore I would never be. The kind of partner my father was. I see the patterns in myself. He passed them to me and I've passed the consequences to people who didn't deserve them.
I was supposed to protect my younger sibling and I didn't. Things happened that I could have intervened in and I was too scared to. What they went through is partly something I failed to stop.

I drove away the person who loved me most because I couldn't believe I deserved to be loved. That's a me problem. But the belief that I don't deserve love is not something I chose. It was taught to me.

I don't know how to have healthy relationships and I accept responsibility for the damage that causes. I also recognize I never had a model for what healthy looks like. The pattern started long before I did anything.

I feel terrible when my partner is upset even when it has nothing to do with me. If she's in a bad mood, I immediately assume I caused it somehow. I spend the whole time trying to figure out what I did.
I feel responsible for my friend's depression deepening. I wasn't there when she really needed me. I was too caught up in my own stuff. She reached out and I wasn't present. That gap matters.

The way I handle money comes from scarcity. I grew up with genuine financial insecurity and the way I think about money now — the anxiety, the hoarding, the inability to spend anything — that is a response to poverty. My parents' poverty.

I've hurt everyone who's gotten close to me in some way and I know it's a pattern. I know patterns mean it's me. But it's also a pattern in the types of people I attract. Where's the line between that being their behavior and mine?
My daughter's estrangement from me is my fault. I know what I did and I know why she made the choice she made. I don't think she's wrong. She's protecting herself from me.
I got into the accident because I was driving while exhausted. I knew I was tired and I drove anyway. The consequences of that choice belong to me.

The way I grew up taught me that my needs were an imposition. My parents were always overwhelmed and I learned to disappear my needs so I wouldn't add to their stress. I still do it. I made myself need-less as a child and now I can't need things as an adult.

I made a comment that I didn't think was harmful and it deeply hurt someone I care about. They explained why it hurt. I understand why now. The impact is real regardless of my intent. What I said caused that hurt.

My best friend is less close to me now and I know it's because I was unavailable during a period when she really needed support. I was there in the past but when it mattered I wasn't. She adjusted accordingly.
My boss is constantly stressed and takes it out on the team. Team morale is the lowest I've seen. Our performance numbers are slipping. All of it tracks back to the environment she creates.
I feel like I have ruined my body. I made choices for years that damaged my health and now my body reflects those choices. My doctor can treat symptoms but the damage was done by my own behavior.

My mother's anxiety shaped our entire household and everything in it. She catastrophized constantly, monitored everything, never allowed anyone to just feel okay. The anxiety I have came directly from the environment she created.
I can't hold a job because every time I start to do well, I do something that undermines it. I push, or I overstep, or I shut down. I know it's me. I can see the pattern. I just can't stop it.

I feel guilty about something that happened years ago and I've never fully let it go. Something I said set off a chain of events that ended with my friend getting hurt. I didn't intend it. But I started it.

My brother is angry with me and has been for years. I know why. I sided with our mother during the divorce when I should have stayed neutral. My relationship with him has never recovered from that choice.
The culture at my job is toxic and it's set by leadership. Every person who leaves, every team that underperforms, every project that fails — it traces back to the values that come from the top.
I failed my friend when she was at her lowest. She came to me and I didn't know what to say and I pulled back. She got through it but not because of anything I did. I just wasn't there.

I've been told I'm too sensitive. I believe that. But where did the sensitivity come from? It came from growing up in an environment where I had to be sensitive to everyone else's moods to stay safe. My sensitivity is not a personality flaw. It's a survival skill I developed because of the people I grew up around.

I let my ex control the financial decisions in our marriage and when we divorced I had nothing. She managed everything and I trusted her. The position I'm in now financially is a result of choices she made that I didn't question.

I know I made my kids feel like they had to earn my love through achievement. I did to them what was done to me. The conditional love cycle passed through me before I understood it. I am responsible for that transmission.
I cut myself off from people I loved because I was ashamed of my addiction. The relationships I lost during those years are on me. I made myself disappear.

I cannot shake the feeling that my partner's bad moods are my fault. Even when she tells me they're not, I track back through the day for what I must have done. I end up apologizing for things she says I didn't do.
My partner is patient but I know I exhaust her. I need more reassurance than anyone should have to give. I know it's too much and I keep needing it anyway. That's my failure.

I've been told my instinct to apologize for everything is exhausting for people. But I developed that instinct in a house where being wrong had consequences. The reflex is not mine originally. It was conditioned into me.
I feel like everything in my family is responding to me. When I'm struggling, everyone struggles. I'm at the center of it and the ripples go outward. I don't want that but I know it's true.

I chose not to reach out when I should have. My friend had told me things were hard and I let the weeks go by without checking in. When she hit rock bottom, I wasn't there. I chose comfort over showing up for her.
My anger destroyed the last relationship. I know that. I know where the anger comes from and understanding that doesn't absolve me of the damage I caused with it.
I feel responsible for other people's emotions in a way that exhausts me. When someone I love is sad, I feel like it must be because of something I did or failed to do.

My ex told me I was responsible for her unhappiness and I believed her. I built my entire sense of self around not failing her. When I still couldn't make her happy I internalized that I was the problem in every relationship I would ever have.

The project failed because our VP kept changing the scope without telling us. By the time we got direction the deadline was impossible. Our careers took a hit for a decision made above us that we had no input into.
I feel guilt about my parents' unhappy marriage. I know they stayed together for me. I was the reason two people were trapped in something neither of them wanted. I've carried that my whole life.
He makes me feel crazy. He says things and then denies them. He tells me I'm too sensitive. He creates the problem and then tells me my reaction to the problem is the problem.
She raised me to need her approval for everything and then withheld it systematically. My inability to self-validate, to feel okay without outside confirmation, was engineered by her parenting.

I can't stop punishing myself for one decision I made five years ago. My therapist says it was understandable given what I knew at the time. But I know what resulted from it. The consequences are evidence of my failure regardless of intent.
My coworker takes all the credit and I let her. I do the work and she presents it. I haven't pushed back once. Whatever recognition I'm not getting is partly my fault for not claiming it.

I feel responsible for my partner's stress. When she has a hard day at work, I think I should have done more at home so she'd have less to deal with when she got back. I always feel like I could have prevented it if I'd just done more.
I know I'm the reason my friendships are shallow. I choose not to go deep. When conversations get too real I redirect. People sense that. They stop going deep with me. That's a wall I built.

I'm responsible for the state of my relationship. I've known what needed to change and I haven't changed it. Not because I can't, but because change is hard and I've been choosing comfort over the work.

I feel like everything bad in my life comes back to choices I made when I was too young or too damaged to know better. But that doesn't mean no one made me this way. Someone made me that damaged person who made those choices.

I spiral into guilt when my partner is unhappy because I automatically scan for what I did. Even when she tells me her sadness has nothing to do with me, I still feel like the source of it somehow. I feel like everything she experiences is a response to me in some way.
My inability to ask for help is causing me to fail slowly. I know help is available and I refuse it because I was taught that needing help makes you weak. Someone decided to teach me that.
I feel like I ruin everything. Relationships, jobs, opportunities. I get to a certain point and something in me destroys it. I know that's mine. I don't know how to stop it.
I feel like my partner's bad moods are something that happens to me. When she's down I feel devastated and anxious and responsible. Her emotions become mine immediately and I feel like I caused them.

I know I disappoint everyone who counts on me. Not because I want to, but because I can't sustain effort. I start strong and then fall apart. People have learned to expect this from me. I did that to their expectations.

When things go wrong I automatically assume it's because I've done something. My therapist says that's not always true. But most of the time when I trace back what went wrong I do find something I did. So it kind of is always true.

I've been carrying blame for my parents' divorce since I was twelve when my mother told me that my needs were what made the marriage impossible. I know intellectually that wasn't true. I still feel it though.

My ex would get angry and tell me everything was my fault and over time I started agreeing with him just to end the argument. Eventually I believed it. Now I'm in a different relationship and I still believe everything is my fault.

I think about her constantly and I know why the relationship ended. I was not ready for what she needed. I wasn't in a place to show up the way she deserved. She knew it before I admitted it. She was right to leave.

When my partner is upset, I feel like she's upset at me even when she says she isn't. I feel responsible for making her feel better. If she's still sad after I've tried to help, I feel like I've failed her.
My children's unhappiness feels like my failure as a parent. When they struggle I immediately think about what I did wrong, what I didn't give them, what wounds I passed on to them.

I've caused harm and I can't let myself stop feeling guilty because feeling guilty feels like the least I can do. Letting go of the guilt would feel like letting myself off the hook for something I genuinely did.
I feel like everyone's unhappiness in my family traces back to me. My parents' stress. My siblings' struggles. I was the kid who needed the most and that need cost everyone else something.
I feel like I'm exhausting my therapist too. She would never say so but I think about it every session. I'm probably her most difficult client.
My negative thinking makes me feel bad and then acting from that bad place makes things worse. I know I cause some of my own problems. I just can't figure out how to interrupt the cycle.
He broke something in me when he left. Not just the relationship but the version of me that existed before. That version is gone. He took it.

I've spent years trying to figure out whether my problems come from what happened to me or from some flaw I was born with. Either way the problems are real and the consequences fall on the people around me as much as on me.
I messed up the project and my team covered for me. They protected my reputation at cost to their time. I owe them something I haven't paid back.

My anxiety is rooted in real events and I understand the causality. Someone did things that made my nervous system permanently altered. My body's responses didn't come from nowhere. They came from what was done to it.
I feel like a burden on everyone who loves me. I need too much reassurance. I require too much patience. The people who stay with me are doing so despite what it costs them.

My ex cheated and I had already known on some level. I stayed because I was afraid to be alone. The hurt I experienced came partly from her betrayal and partly from my own choice to stay when I knew. I can't separate those two things cleanly.

I feel anxious when people seem happy around me because I think I'm going to do something to ruin it. I keep waiting for myself to mess it up because that's what I do. I'm the one who makes things go wrong.
I know I'm the one who pushes people away right when they get too close. I can see myself doing it. I can narrate it while it happens. But I can't stop it and eventually they go and that's on me.
My sister called and I could tell she was struggling and I kept the call short because I was tired. I've been feeling terrible about it ever since. She reaches out so rarely and I brushed her off.
I feel devastated and I blame myself because I feel like I must have done something to cause it. Maybe I did. Maybe I didn't. But the feeling is the same either way.
She's unhappy and I feel the weight of that as if I caused it directly. She says I didn't but I feel like the cause and the effect are me. If I was different, everything would be different.

I feel bad and I assume it means I've done something wrong. My therapist says sometimes you just feel bad. I know that intellectually. But when I feel bad I always look for the thing I did to deserve it.
Something is wrong with my partner today and I've been going over every interaction we've had in the last week trying to figure out what I did. She said it's not me. I don't believe her.

I feel like I'm making her unhappy just by being who I am. Not by doing anything specific. Just by being the particular person I am, which is not quite right for her, not quite enough. That must be my fault.

I think I'm making my kids anxious just by being around them. My anxiety lives in the house like another presence. They absorb it. I am causing my children psychological harm just by existing in their space.
I feel guilty that my relationship is good. Like I haven't earned a healthy relationship. Like I'll eventually ruin this one too because that's what I do to good things.

I feel like I'm the reason things go wrong even when I wasn't there. If I had been available, if I had answered the phone, if I had stayed instead of leaving, things would have turned out differently. I'm responsible for outcomes I wasn't present for.

I trust no one and I know who taught me that. Multiple people showed me what happens when you trust. I drew the rational conclusion from the evidence they gave me. They made trusting others feel too dangerous.
I feel like my friend is drifting away and I've been trying to figure out what I did. I look at every text I sent, every time I cancelled, every time I was distracted. I know it's me. It's always me.
I feel terrible because I'm in a bad mood and I know it affects everyone around me. I feel responsible for the atmosphere in the room. If I'm off, everyone suffers for it.

I'm in a bad mood and I know I caused it by ruminating. I chose to keep thinking about it. I made myself feel worse. I know I do this. I feel responsible for my own suffering and for what that suffering costs the people around me.
She said she was having a hard time and I feel like I should fix it. Not because she asked me to. Because I feel like her hard time is connected to me somehow and that means I owe her a solution.

I know my bad childhood explains my struggles. I also know that explanation doesn't help the people affected by my struggles. Understanding the source doesn't remove the responsibility for the impact.
My partner seems distant lately and I feel it and I feel responsible for it. I don't know what I did but the distance feels like my fault. Distance always is.

I feel like I'm responsible for everything and that if things go wrong it's because of some failure of mine. I can't stop believing that. Even when evidence says otherwise, I look for the way it's still somehow my fault.

I made her cry. I don't even fully know what I said but I made her cry and the image of her face is the only thing I can think about. It doesn't matter what I was trying to say. The impact is what matters and I caused it.
I feel like if I disappeared from people's lives things would go better for them. Not because I want to disappear. Because I genuinely believe I make things harder for the people I love.

My ex's depression got worse during our relationship. I don't know how much of that was me and how much was other factors. But I know I wasn't helping. Whatever I contributed to that worsening is mine to own.
I feel like I ruined the evening because of my mood. Everyone else was having a good time and I brought the energy down. I couldn't make myself be okay and everyone felt it.
She's upset and I feel it as if it's happening to me. When she hurts, I feel like I caused it and I feel it simultaneously. Her pain becomes my pain and my guilt at the same time.

I can't stop apologizing. I apologize for things I didn't do because it still feels true that I'm responsible. My therapist says I take on too much responsibility for things outside my control. I don't know how to feel the difference.
I feel like I'm at the center of everything that goes wrong. In my relationship, in my family, at work. When something goes wrong I look for my role in it first, even before I know what happened.

My therapist keeps asking me to consider that not everything is my fault. I hear the question and immediately start building the case for why it still is. It's faster and more automatic than any other thinking I do.

I can feel when my partner's upset and I immediately know I did it. I just know. My therapist says this isn't always accurate. But I feel it so strongly as fact that the feeling is more convincing than anything she says.

I feel guilty about things that happened before I was born. I know that's irrational. But when I hear about things my family did, I feel responsible for it because I'm part of the family. The guilt feels like mine even when the action wasn't.
I feel bad about myself and I immediately look for the thing I did wrong that made me deserve to feel this way. When I feel bad I must have done something bad. That's how it works for me.

I know my mother is unhappy and I feel like her unhappiness is connected to me. To my choices, my distance, my failure to be what she needed me to be. Her life went differently than she wanted and I'm part of why.
She left me and I feel like I deserved it. I know the things I did that drove her away. I think I probably drove her away on purpose somehow even though I didn't want her to go.

I feel this constant low hum of guilt. It doesn't attach to anything specific most of the time. It's just there. I must have done something. There must be something I'm responsible for. There always is.

I feel like my unhappiness is contagious and that being around me makes people sad. I don't feel that's irrational. I think it's actually true. I change the energy of rooms I enter when I'm like this.

My friend was going through something and she didn't tell me and I wonder now if it's because she knew I wouldn't handle it well. If she didn't reach out because she couldn't trust me with it, that's something I did.

I feel responsible for the relationship not working out. I can point to specific things I should have done differently. I know it wasn't all me. But enough of it was that the proportionate ownership still feels like too much to carry.

I feel my partner's pain as if it's something I caused even when she has explicitly told me I didn't. She tells me. I believe her for about three minutes and then the feeling that I did it comes back.
I feel like I am the source of everyone's stress around me. If I were calmer, more capable, more put together, the people who love me wouldn't have to work so hard.

I feel like I caused the tension in the room even though nothing was said to me. My therapist says I'm hypervigilant to social cues. But I don't think I'm misreading them. I think I'm correctly reading that I'm the problem.

He's having a hard week and I feel guilty about it even though I know it's work stress and has nothing to do with me. I still feel like I should have done something to protect him from it or prepared him better or been more supportive before it started.
My friend seems distant and I feel it as guilt before I even know what happened. Before I investigate, I assume I did something. That's where my brain starts.
I feel like my existence is a weight on the people I love. Not because they've said so. Because I can see what it costs them to be patient with me.
He told me the relationship ending was mutual and that he held responsibility too. I heard that. I retained none of it. In my mind the end of the relationship is mine to own entirely.
I feel responsible for making people around me happy and when they're not I feel like I failed at my job. I know I don't have a job to keep people happy. It still feels like I do.

She is sad and I feel the sadness as accusation even when no accusation was made. Feeling it that way makes me defensive and then my defensiveness probably does make things worse. I participate in creating the very outcome I was afraid of.
I feel terrible and I know it must be because I've done something wrong. That's the logic I always use. Bad feelings require bad causes, and the cause is usually me.
I feel responsible for my partner's anxiety spikes. When she gets anxious I think back to the last thing I said or did and wonder if I triggered it. She says that's not how it works. I still check.

I believe that if I were better, everyone around me would be better. More patient, more capable, less damaged. The quality of my relationships is a reflection of the quality of person I am, which is not good enough.

I feel guilty when my partner has a hard day at work even though I know I have nothing to do with her job. I feel like I should have done something to make her personal life easier so the work difficulty would land on a better foundation. The fact that I didn't is on me.
I feel like I make things worse just by being in them. Not because I try to. Just because who I am and how I function has a negative drag on everything I'm part of.

Something went wrong and I feel like I should have predicted it and stopped it. Even when I had no information that would have allowed me to predict it. I still feel that the right version of me would have known.
I feel like I'm the cause when things go wrong and an accident when things go right. The good outcomes are luck. The bad ones are me.

My partner is going through something and all I keep thinking about is what I should be doing differently to fix it for her. She hasn't asked me to fix anything. I just feel responsible for her pain automatically.

When she's upset I feel like her feelings are a verdict on me. Whatever she's feeling, it's a judgment on whether I'm adequate. Bad feelings in people I love feel like they have something to say about me.
I feel like a bad person and I'm trying to figure out what I did to confirm that feeling. Something must have happened. I feel this bad, so I must have done something this bad.
I feel guilty about my anger and I feel guilty about being sad and I feel guilty when I'm okay because someone around me isn't. I've outsourced my emotional permission to everyone else around me.
My therapist asked me to consider what it would feel like to not be responsible for other people's feelings. I couldn't picture it. I don't know what that version of me feels like.
I feel bad and I feel certain it's deserved. Feeling bad for no reason doesn't make sense to me. If I feel bad, I must have earned it.
I feel like the people in my life would be happier if they had someone else. Not because they've said that. Because I know the burden I am, and I know they deserve better.

I feel like I have caused my own suffering. Not just contributed to it. Caused it. The choices I've made, the patterns I repeat, the walls I build — all mine. I can't blame anyone for the situation I'm in.

I feel guilty about everything always. I don't know what a clean conscience feels like anymore. There's always something I should have done differently and if I feel it strongly enough, it must mean I did something wrong.
I feel her unhappiness and I feel responsible for it and I feel the weight of that responsibility as something I deserve to carry. She's unhappy. That's connected to me. That's my burden.
I feel like I should be doing more. More for my partner, more for my kids, more at work. Whatever I'm doing, the gap between that and what I should be doing is my failure.

I feel like I cause pain without trying to and I feel responsible for that even though I didn't intend it. Intent doesn't matter when the impact is real. I know I've hurt people even when I was trying to help.
She didn't say it was my fault but her tone said something. I felt it. I know that look. When she's like that, it always comes back to something I did. I just don't know what yet.

My partner's sadness feels like something I did to her and that feeling is instant and overwhelming even when I know logically I didn't cause it. The emotional logic says I'm to blame. The rational logic says I'm not. The emotional logic wins.
I feel bad when people around me are sad because their sadness feels like information about me. About my worth, my impact, my value. I can't separate what they feel from what I am.

I feel like I'm a problem that people are managing rather than a person they're choosing. I feel responsible for making myself manageable. And when I'm not, I feel like I've failed the people around me.

She's struggling and all I can think about is what I should have noticed earlier, what I should have said, what I should have done. She's struggling because something in me missed the signs and didn't act quickly enough.
I feel like everything that goes wrong in my orbit is mine to fix and mine to feel bad about. Even when I'm not the cause. I automatically become responsible in my own mind.

I feel guilty when I'm happy because I know people in my life are struggling. My good mood feels like abandonment of people who aren't in a good place. So I'm responsible even for my own wellbeing in the context of theirs.

When she seems less warm I immediately think I must have done something. I review the last 24 hours. I find evidence. I always find something I could have done better. The case against me is always available.

I trust no one fully and the people who suffer for that are the ones trying to love me. My inability to trust is a wound from somewhere else and the people I love pay the cost of someone else's damage.

She lost her job and I feel guilty. I don't know why. She doesn't blame me. But I feel like maybe if I had been more supportive during a time she was stressed, things would have gone differently for her at work.

I know I made my anxiety worse by not treating it sooner. I knew something was wrong for years and I avoided getting help. The severity of where it is now is partly a result of my choice to ignore it.
I feel like I've infected the people close to me with my unhappiness. That they were lighter before they knew me. I absorb good energy from people and give them heavy things in return.

I feel guilty and I assume there's a reason and if I look hard enough I'll find what I did. My therapist says sometimes guilt is just anxiety in disguise. But if I feel guilty, something must have happened. I must have done something to someone.

I feel like the relationships in my life are structured around managing me. Around accommodating my needs and my limitations. I cost more than I contribute and the people who stay are paying a price I can't repay.
I feel like I've poisoned every relationship I've ever been in. Not intentionally. Just by being the person I am and doing the things I inevitably do. The pattern is undeniable at this point.

I feel responsible for my friend's current situation because I introduced her to the person who hurt her. I brought him into her life. Whatever happened after that introduction is something I put into motion.
She told me her bad mood wasn't about me and I felt relief for about thirty seconds before the familiar feeling came back. It is about me. It's always about me. She's just being kind.
I worry that I make my partner feel trapped. Not because she's said that. Because I know what it's like to be with someone who needs too much, and I know that I am that person.
He hurt me and he should feel bad about it. I've forgiven him to his face. Inside I haven't and I don't know if I ever will. The hurt he caused me is his and I'm still carrying it.

My relationship ended and I've spent months going over my role in it. I've accounted for everything I did wrong. I've barely thought about what he did. I focus entirely on my part because the idea of blaming him feels like weakness.

I feel responsible for my partner's unhappiness even when she says I'm not the cause. I hear that she's struggling and I feel immediately accountable. She hasn't asked me to fix it. My nervous system already decided I must.

I feel guilty about something that happened to someone else in a situation I had no control over. I wasn't there. I couldn't have changed it. I still think about what I could have done if I had been. The guilt doesn't require my presence or my involvement. It finds me anyway.
I feel guilty when my partner has a good day too. Like I'm waiting for my role in ruining it. Like the good day is just a setup for something I'll eventually do wrong.

I feel like I'm performing stability for the people I love while underneath I'm barely holding it together. The gap between how I present and who I am is my deception. Their trust in me is based on a lie I live every day.

My partner is sad and I feel the sadness as accusation and guilt simultaneously. I know intellectually that her sadness is about her. Emotionally it lands as a verdict on me. The verdict is always guilty.
I feel responsible for the accident even though I didn't cause it. I was the one who had planned the trip. I'd suggested the route. Something bad happened because I put people in that situation.

I feel responsible for my own trauma. I know that doesn't make rational sense. But I feel like a person who was functioning properly would have found a way to not be affected so deeply by what happened. The depth of the wound is evidence of some weakness in me.
I feel like I'm ruining my kids by not being okay. My anxiety, my low days, my inability to always be present — they see it. I am teaching them a template for suffering.
I always find a way to make things harder than they need to be. I complicate things. I create problems. Whatever difficulty exists in my life, I've earned it by making unnecessary choices.

I feel bad about the past and I can't let myself feel better about it because the past happened and the people I hurt are still out there somewhere carrying what I did. My comfort doesn't erase their experience.
I feel like my partner deserves someone who doesn't need this much. I feel responsible for her having settled for me when she could have had someone easier to love.

I feel guilty when I'm enjoying something because something bad happened recently and I feel like enjoying myself is a betrayal of the seriousness of what happened. I feel like my good mood implicates me somehow.
I feel like my bad days are a failure I'm inflicting on the people who love me. They signed up for a relationship, not for managing my mental health. Every bad day is something I'm doing to them.
My relationship is going through something and I feel like I'm to blame even before I know what the problem is. My default is to assume culpability and work backward from there.

I know she's unhappy and I feel the weight of it on me as responsibility. It's my fault she's unhappy, it's my fault I can't fix it, and it's my fault she has to feel that way instead of something better.

I feel guilty about everything in my life and I feel responsible for everyone in my life and I don't know how to feel otherwise. The only question I ask myself is what did I do wrong, not whether I did anything wrong.
I'm responsible for causing pain to people I love even when they say I'm not. Their absolution doesn't change what I know I did. Forgiveness doesn't make the harm less real.

I feel responsible for my own loneliness because I've kept everyone at a distance. I know why I did it. But knowing why doesn't change the fact that I chose it and now I'm here alone with a bunch of choices that led to this.

I feel like everyone around me would be fine if I just got it together. The fact that I haven't gotten it together yet is an active choice I'm making at some level. I could fix this if I really tried.
My mood brings down the people around me and I know it. When I'm having a hard day, my partner has a hard day. That's my fault. My mental health is a contagion I expose the people I love to.
I'm not able to get better and I feel like it's my fault. Like if I were trying hard enough, I would be better by now. The fact that I'm still struggling means I haven't done enough.

I feel guilty about getting help because the resources I'm using could go to someone who deserves them more. I'm taking up space in a system with limited capacity and I don't know that I've earned that space.

I feel responsible for what happened even though I was told it wasn't my fault. The people telling me that love me and they would say that regardless. The facts are what they are and my role in them is what it is.

I feel like I burden every room I enter with my sadness. I try to manage it before seeing people but I know it leaks through and I know they feel it. I'm an emotional burden that people are too polite to name.

I don't know how to stop feeling responsible for everything. I've been told it's disproportionate. I've been told to work on it. I've been working on it for years. The feeling that everything is my fault is faster than any coping strategy I've developed.
I feel like the problems in my relationship exist because I'm in it. A different partner would have a different experience. The problems track me, not the relationship itself.

I caused someone pain and they've forgiven me and moved on and I haven't. Not because I want to hold onto it but because letting go feels like saying it didn't matter. The guilt is the only way I know to acknowledge the harm I did.
I feel responsible for the distance that's grown between me and my friends. I know I've been unavailable. I know I've been inside my own head. The friendship atrophied because of my absence.
I feel like if I disappeared from the picture, the people I love would find things easier. I'm the variable. Remove the variable and the outcomes improve.
I feel like I'm always the reason something goes wrong and never the reason something goes right. Successes happen in spite of me. Failures happen because of me.
My therapist says it's not all my fault. I feel like saying that is her job. I feel like she has to say that. I don't feel like it changes the truth of what I know I did.
She said her bad mood has nothing to do with me. I nodded. Internally I spent the next four hours identifying what I must have done.

I feel guilty about my existence in a way that's hard to describe. Not that I want to not exist. Just that my presence costs things. My presence in people's lives, in the world, costs things that I haven't earned the right to cost.
I'm in a bad mood and I feel guilty that I'm in a bad mood because my bad mood makes other people's days harder. I feel responsible for the radius of my own emotional state.
I feel bad about myself and then I feel guilty for feeling bad about myself because feeling bad affects the people around me and that means my feelings are a burden I'm imposing on them.
I know why I turned out this way. I can trace it back. That tracing back doesn't reduce the consequences. I am what I am because of what happened to me and the people around me deal with what I am.

Something went badly in my relationship and I immediately felt that it was my fault. She said it wasn't. I know intellectually she might be right. But I feel the fault as mine and the feeling is more compelling than the reasoning.
I am angry at what was done to me and I don't feel I should have to hide that anger or manage it away. The anger is appropriate. It belongs to the person who caused it.
I feel like I ruin things. Not because I'm malicious. Because who I am, the patterns I carry, the way I function — these things damage what I'm part of. It's not intentional. It's structural.

I feel guilty about needing help and then guilty about not asking for it and then guilty about asking for too much once I do. I don't know what the right amount of need is but I know I'm always getting it wrong.

My ex ruined my ability to trust and I haven't fully rebuilt it in the relationships since. Everyone I've dated since him has had to deal with the wreckage he left behind. That's not fair to them. It's not fair to me either. But it's where we are.
I feel like a drain on everyone who cares about me. Not because they've said that. Because I know what I require and I know what people can reasonably give and the math doesn't work in my favor.

I believe I caused my own problems through my choices and I can't stop punishing myself for it even though the punishment changes nothing. I just feel like I don't deserve to feel better about what I did.
I feel like my sadness is something I'm doing to my partner. Like my bad days are a form of action against her happiness. She didn't sign up for this much sadness and I can't stop having it.
I feel responsible for my friends' feelings. I feel guilty when they're unhappy and anxious about whether I contributed. I scan every interaction for ways I could have made things worse.

I feel like everything wrong in my life is a reflection of my choices, my limitations, my failures. When I try to identify what external factors contributed I can't hold onto them. The self-blame is faster.

I accept full responsibility for the things I did. I just also wish that someone had done less damage to me before I did them, because the damage done to me is part of the chain of causation even if it doesn't excuse what came after.

I made a mistake and I've forgiven myself in principle. The body hasn't forgiven me. The guilt is in my chest and in my sleep and in the automatic apology that comes out before I even know what I'm apologizing for.

I feel responsible for outcomes I wasn't involved in. For people I haven't seen in years. If something goes wrong for someone I care about, even from a distance, even when I couldn't have known, I feel like a better version of me would have done something.
I feel like the source of my family's pain. My mental illness, my struggles, my needs — they've shaped the family around me and not for the better. They've organized their lives to manage me.

I feel guilty all the time. I don't have a specific thing I'm guilty about most of the time. The guilt is ambient. I must be doing something wrong. The feeling is too consistent to not be pointing at something real.
I feel responsible for everything and insufficient at everything and the gap between those two things is where I live.
My partner is struggling today and all I feel is that I caused it somehow. That's where I go first. She tells me I didn't. I hear her. I go back there anyway.

My partner seems fine but I keep feeling like something is wrong and that I'm the cause. The feeling is stronger than any evidence to the contrary. Even when everything is clearly okay, I feel like I must be missing something I did wrong.

When I feel bad I assume I did something wrong. When I feel guilty I assume there's a reason for it. I feel guilty constantly. By this logic I'm constantly doing something wrong and I'm probably right.

I feel like my suffering is self-inflicted even when it isn't. I feel like I could get out of it if I tried hard enough. The fact that I'm still here means I must not be trying hard enough. That's my fault.
I feel responsible for my partner's emotions and I feel responsible for fixing them. She hasn't asked me to fix them. The responsibility is something I impose on myself and then fail at repeatedly.

I did something wrong and I know what it was and I can't move past it. I keep returning to it. My therapist calls it rumination. I call it accountability. I feel like stopping the rumination would mean saying what I did didn't matter.
I feel responsible for my own sadness in a way that makes it worse. I feel sad and then I feel guilty for being sad and then I feel sad about the guilt and the loop continues.
I feel bad and I feel like feeling bad is my fault. I feel guilty about struggling. I feel like a person who was doing things right wouldn't be struggling this much.
I feel like my anxiety is a choice at some level. Like I could decide not to be anxious if I was stronger. The fact that I'm anxious means I'm not strong enough. That's on me.
I feel like I've failed everyone I love by being this way. Not by doing anything specific. Just by being a person with these limitations who loves people who deserve someone without them.

I don't know how to stop taking responsibility for things outside my control. My therapist has given me tools. The tools work sometimes. But the default is blame, specifically self-blame, and defaults are faster than tools.
My partner had a bad dream about me. She woke up upset and I felt guilty. I know I can't control her dreams. I still felt like something I did must have seeded it.
I feel like I'm the constant in a recurring equation and the output of the equation keeps being pain. At some point you stop blaming the inputs and start looking at the constant.

I feel responsible for things I have no control over because I feel like if I were different, more present, more capable, less broken, those things wouldn't have happened. The logic isn't sound. The feeling is very sound.
She cried and I felt it as evidence of something I failed at. Not failure in a specific action. Failure as a person. Her tears feel like a judgment on what I am.

I feel like if I feel bad, I've earned it. My suffering is a fair consequence of my failures. I know my therapist would push back on that. I still feel like I haven't earned the right to feel better yet.

I feel like I'm the cause of most of what goes wrong in my life and in the lives of the people near me. I say this not as a catastrophe but as an accurate reading of the evidence I've collected over a lifetime.

I feel responsible for how I am. I know that some of how I am came from things done to me. I still feel like I should have been able to rise above that. The fact that I haven't is my failure, even if it was someone else who created the condition.

I feel like I owe my partner more than I'm giving. I feel like she's being cheated by being with me. She doesn't see it that way. I feel responsible for the gap between what she deserves and what I can provide.

I feel guilty for existing in the way I exist. For needing the things I need. For affecting people the way I affect them. I don't know what the right level of existence is, but I feel like I'm consistently over it.

My partner is in a bad mood and I immediately start scanning myself. What did I say, what didn't I do, what have I been doing that's making her life harder. The scan produces results every time. I'm always able to find something.

I feel like I've been a burden to the people who love me my whole life. I've needed too much. I've cost too much. The love I've received from people who stayed is something I owe more than I can ever repay.

I feel guilty when things are going well because I know things only seem fine because I haven't messed it up yet. The good period isn't a reflection of how things are. It's a reflection of how long it's been since I last made things worse.
I feel responsible for my own unhappiness and for the unhappiness of everyone who is connected to me. I'm the source. I know I'm the source. I just don't know how to be a different source.
I feel guilty for not being better. Not for anything specific. Just for still being this version of me when I should be further along by now.

I caused pain by doing what I did and the people I love are carrying it. I am responsible for that. The fact that I've changed or grown since then doesn't erase the reality of what they went through because of me.
I feel like I'm a problem that love has not been able to solve. And that's a failure of my adequacy as a person, not a failure of the love.

I feel like I'm responsible for my current state of mind and that feeling bad is something I've done to myself. Even when I know better intellectually, the emotional logic says I'm not trying hard enough to feel better.
I feel bad and I feel it must be my fault and I feel guilty about feeling bad and I feel guilty about the guilt and somewhere in this loop I lost track of whether any of this is proportionate.
